data.title: Warren, Wyden press UI vendors over alleged failures in unemployment systems
data.description:
U.S. Sens. Elizabeth Warren and Ron Wyden have sent a series of sharply worded letters to major government technology vendors, raising concerns that their systems may be disrupting unemployment benefits and demanding detailed answers about how those platforms operate.

The letters, dated April 5 and sent to executives at Deloitte, Thomson Reuters (TRI) and Geographic Solutions, argue that states’ growing reliance on private contractors to modernize unemployment insurance systems has created new risks for jobless Americans trying to access benefits.

Lawmakers question ‘black box’ systems

In the letters, the senators describe a system increasingly dependent on outside vendors, many of whom provide off-the-shelf software or AI-driven tools that states have limited ability to audit or customize.

They warn that these systems often function as opaque ‘black boxes,’ with vendors sometimes declining to disclose how eligibility or fraud determinations are made, citing proprietary technology.

That lack of transparency, the lawmakers argue, can have serious consequences when automated systems make mistakes, particularly when state officials defer to algorithmic decisions without fully reviewing them.

Automation, AI under scrutiny

A major focus of the inquiry is the growing use of artificial intelligence in unemployment systems, including tools that verify identity, flag fraud and assess eligibility.

The senators say such systems have produced errors ranging from incorrectly flagging legitimate claims as fraudulent to misinterpreting basic information in applications.

They also cite reports of glitches that delay or halt payments, as well as cases where minor discrepancies or shared addresses triggered fraud alerts, potentially cutting off benefits for eligible workers.

While automation can streamline administrative work, the lawmakers emphasize that federal law requires human oversight in eligibility determinations, warning that overreliance on AI could undermine that standard.

Past system failures highlighted

The letters point to a history of troubled technology rollouts.

For Deloitte, the senators reference prior state projects that experienced technical failures, including system outages and processing errors that delayed payments for large numbers of claimants.

In the case of Thomson Reuters (TRI), the inquiry highlights concerns around fraud-detection software developed by its subsidiary Pondera Solutions, which has faced complaints alleging it led to wrongful denials or reductions of public benefits.

For Geographic Solutions, lawmakers cite a 2022 cyber incident that disrupted unemployment and job-search systems across multiple states, as well as earlier implementation issues that created administrative backlogs.

Broad request for data and accountability

The senators are seeking extensive information about how these companies design, test and maintain their systems, including what data their algorithms rely on, how errors are identified and corrected, and whether states have visibility into or control over the technology.

They also asked for details on contract values, performance outcomes and whether modernization efforts have improved payment accuracy and timeliness.

Responses are due by April 23.

Stakes for workers

At the center of the inquiry is the role unemployment benefits play as a financial safety net. The senators argue that when systems fail, whether due to coding errors, flawed data or opaque algorithms, the consequences can be immediate and severe, affecting recipients’ ability to pay for housing, food and healthcare.

The letters signal growing scrutiny in Washington over the intersection of public benefits and private-sector technology, particularly as states continue to modernize systems that millions of Americans rely on during periods of joblessness.

Seeking Alpha is asking for comment from the vendors.

data.title: JPMorgan sticks with its view that Tesla shares will crash
data.description:
Longtime Tesla (TSLA) bear JPMorgan reiterated its Underweight rating on the EV and autonomy play on Monday ahead of the company's first-quarter earnings report.

Analyst Ryan Brinkman pointed to Tesla's (TSLA) underwhelming Q1 deliveries report and year-over-year decline in energy storage installations. Brinkman and his team now expect Tesla (TSLA) to report Q1 EPS of $0.30 vs. the prior estimate of $0.43 and consensus mark of $0.38.

"We continue to see large -60% downside to our $145 December 2026 price target and advise investors to approach TSLA shares with a high degree of caution, mindful of execution risk and the time value of money within the context of the materially stronger distant out-year earnings expectations implied by the rise in TSLA share price that has occurred alongside a material collapse in consensus for all performance metrics through at least the end of the decade," advised Brinkman. He noted that "incredibly," TSLA shares are  more than 50% higher now than when delivery volume peaked in June 2022, despite the sluggish deliveries growth. 

Looking ahead, JPMorgan thinks the investment positives for Tesla (TSLA) of its highly differentiated business model, appealing product portfolio, and leading-edge technology are more than offset by above-average execution risk, rising competition, growing controversy in regard to the brand, and valuation that seems to be pricing in a lot.

data.title: Strait of Hormuz traffic highest in weeks, but Qatar LNG vessels make U-turn
data.description:
Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has increased to its highest levels since the early days of the Iran war, as India, Iraq, and other countries secured apparent safe-passage agreements, Bloomberg reported Monday.

The strait saw 21 ships transit over the weekend—the highest two-day total since the first days of March, when traffic was declining—as more energy-starved governments apparently made deals to extract vessels, cargoes, and crews from the Persian Gulf.

India, which has negotiated the exit of some ships, has seen eight of its liquefied petroleum gas tankers come through Hormuz; the Green Asha, which reportedly transited the strait on Sunday, is the first midsize gas carrier carrying LPG to cross since the start of the Middle East war, with previous LPG vessels all very large gas carriers.

While Iran is negotiating with friendly countries, the deal terms remain opaque, even when the agreements are publicly recognized, as with Iraq over the weekend, and are even more so in cases where it is unclear which counterparties secured safe passage, as with ships linked to France and Japan.

However, two vessels loaded with liquefied natural gas from Qatar's Ras Laffan, Qatar, U-turned away after moving eastward towards the Strait ​of Hormuz, ship-tracking data showed Monday.

So far, no loaded LNG tanker has passed through Hormuz since the U.S. and Israel began strikes on Iran in late February.

Qatar is the world's second-largest exporter of ​LNG, but Iranian attacks have knocked out 17% of the country's LNG export capacity, with repairs expected to sideline 12.8M ​tons/year of the fuel for ​3-5 years.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

data.title: Kratos shares jump after analyst upgrade points to hypersonics upside
data.description:
Shares of Kratos Defense & Security Solutions (KTOS) surged as much as 8.6% on Monday, extending earlier gains after a bullish analyst call highlighted accelerating growth prospects tied to next-generation weapons programs.

The rally follows an upgrade from Jefferies, which lifted its rating on the defense contractor to buy from hold. In a note to clients, analyst Sheila Kahyaoglu pointed to a potential upside scenario in which Kratos’ Government Solutions segment delivers compound annual revenue growth of roughly 31% through 2028.

That outlook is underpinned by increasing demand tied to hypersonic systems, an area where Kratos has been expanding its footprint. The company’s turbine technologies unit is also positioned to benefit from rising investment in missile propulsion, including its involvement in the Prometheus joint venture, according to the note.

Kahyaoglu additionally flagged an anticipated production ramp for Kratos’ Valkyrie unmanned drone program, another contributor to the company’s longer-term growth trajectory.

Jefferies maintained its $85 price target on the stock, implying roughly 26% upside from Thursday’s closing level.

data.title: Dow downgraded at BofA amid rally driven by temporary earnings surge
data.description:
Dow Inc. (DOW) shares may have run too far, too fast, according to a new research report from Bank of America Securities. Analysts led by Matthew DeYoe on Monday downgraded the chemical maker to Underperform from a previous investment rating of Neutral.

The call comes after a sharp rally in the shares, which analysts say is being fueled by short-term market disruptions rather than sustainable improvements in underlying fundamentals.

Rally driven by conflict, not fundamentals

The report points to the ongoing geopolitical conflict involving Iran as a key driver behind surging petrochemical prices, which in turn have boosted Dow’s (DOW) earnings outlook. Prices for key inputs such as ethylene have spiked dramatically, creating a temporary window of elevated margins for producers.

However, BofA analysts caution that these gains are unlikely to persist. While Dow (DOW) is expected to benefit from stronger pricing through mid-2026, the report forecasts that chemical prices will begin to decline later in the year as supply chains normalize and disruptions ease.

Earnings surge expected to fade

BofA significantly raised its near-term earnings estimates, projecting 2026 earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of nearly $6 billion, up sharply from prior forecasts due to higher margins and tighter supply conditions.

Even so, the firm argues that investors are placing too much weight on these elevated earnings levels. The report suggests that markets are effectively pricing in an extended period of “overearning,” whereas analysts expect peak conditions to last only a few quarters before reverting to more typical levels.

Structural oversupply remains a concern

Beyond the near-term boost, the report highlights deeper challenges facing the global petrochemical industry. A wave of new capacity, particularly in China, is expected to outpace demand growth for several years, pushing utilization rates lower and weighing on pricing power.

According to the analysis, global supply growth could require massive production cuts to rebalance the market, a scenario analysts view as unlikely. Without such adjustments, the industry is expected to return to a state of oversupply once current disruptions subside.

Valuation reflects overly optimistic outlook

At current levels, BofA said Dow’s (DOW) stock price implies a longer duration of elevated earnings than is realistic. The report estimates that the market is pricing in roughly a year and a half of peak profitability, well beyond the firm’s expectation of a shorter-lived cycle.

To reflect this view, analysts lowered the valuation multiple applied to future earnings, even as they raised near-term forecasts. The resulting $35 price target represents downside from recent trading levels around $41.

Cost cuts offer partial cushion

Dow’s (DOW) internal cost-reduction efforts could help soften the impact of declining commodity prices in later years. The report models roughly $1 billion in productivity improvements through 2028, which may help stabilize earnings as market conditions normalize.

Still, BofA maintains that these measures are not enough to offset broader industry headwinds, including weak demand, rising supply, and the eventual unwinding of conflict-driven price spikes.

In sum, while Dow (DOW) may enjoy a near-term earnings boost, the report argues that the longer-term outlook remains constrained, leaving the stock vulnerable after its recent run-up.

data.title: EHC gains as Street applauds CMS payment rate increase
data.description:
• Shares of Encompass Health (EHC) are ~6% higher in Monday trading after several Street analysts said they view favorably a proposed CMS payment rate increase for inpatient rehabilitation hospital operators in fiscal 2027.
  • Those payments are slated to rise by 2.4%, though Keybanc's Matthew Gilmor, who rates Encompass at overweight, said the agency could bump it a bit higher with the final rule.
  • He noted that "concerns regarding the FY27 proposal have been an overhang on EHC’s stock and getting past this event could set a foundation for outperformance in 2H."
  • Leerink's Whit Mayo, who has an outperform rating, added "there is no mention of any policy around the home health transfer rule which has driven some investor anxiety ahead of the annual rulemaking cycle."

data.title: Northern Trust upgraded to Outperform at BMO as wealth focus bodes well for earnings
data.description:
BMO Capital Markets upgraded Northern Trust (NTRS) to Outperform from Market Perform on Monday as the bank's shift to wealth and asset management combined with productivity initiatives begins to fuel earnings growth.

Northern Trust's (NTRS) wealth management business produces pretax margins of ~38%, compared with asset servicing's ~24%, as management spurs growth through its Family Office Solutions targeting $100M-$750M clients. "Momentum is building, with record GFO wins and NTAM product innovation across ETFs, alternatives, and custom SMAs (separately managed accounts) supporting double-digit earnings growth," analyst Brennan Hawken wrote in a note to clients.

Meanwhile, the bank now plans expenses around productivity key production indicators instead of static budgets. "After investing ~$3B in technology over three years, the firm is entering the payback phase," Hawken said. "Expense-to-trust fee ratios have declined for four straight quarters, and 2026 guidance (<5% expense growth vs. low- to mid-single-digit fee growth) implies continued operating leverage."

Northern Trust's (NTRS) CET1 at 12.6%, well above its 7.0% regulatory minimum and its 11%-12% internal target, provides flexibility for elevated capital return, the analyst added. Moreover, the bank's stake in Visa (V) class B-2 stock is worth ~$1.18B ($550M proceeds) and can be monetized once litigation clears, he noted. Previous proceeds were used for “modernization” efforts, which increased expenses. "This time, we expect a pivot toward buybacks and selective wealth management tuck-ins, reflecting a disciplined capital allocation framework and muted appetite for incremental operating investment," Hawken said.

Separately, Goldman Sachs upgraded Northern Trust (NTRS) to Neutral from Sell, citing an improved outlook for net interest income, helped by stronger-than-expected deposit balances and "a more favorable rates trajectory."

Northern Trust (NTRS) stock rose 0.4% in Monday late morning trading.

data.title: Analog chip suppliers seeing 'meaningful improvement' in demand: KeyBanc
data.description:
Analog semiconductor companies, such as Texas Instruments (TXN) or Analog Devices (ADI), are seeing a “meaningful improvement” in demand for several reasons beyond artificial intelligence, investment firm KeyBanc Capital Markets said.

Among the reasons are a rise in lead times due to tight supply and demand being full forward in certain markets, such as PCs and smartphones, to get ahead of rising memory prices.

Additionally, Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) has started to cut support for lagging-edge capacity as it looks to add more chip-on-a-wafer-substrate supply. Lastly, the non-AI portion of the analog market is “increasingly” competing for resources when compared to AI, especially in power management, where Nvidia's (NVDA) GPUs have seen an average of a 40% increase in power requirement needs from generation to generation.

“This is leading to broad-based price increases across semiconductors, as many power analog suppliers [ON, IFX, MPWR, Renesas (RNS), AOSL] have announced price increases particularly within power management,” KeyBanc analyst John Vinh wrote in a note to clients.

As such, there are positive implications for companies across the space, including TI, Analog Devices, Microchip (MCHP), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), and Onsemi (ON).

“Positive implications include: 1) Channel booking trends are seeing a meaningful improvement given increasing lead times and supply tightness; 2) Tightness in supply in part driven by outsized AI demand is driving broad-based price increases particularly within analog power,” Vinh added.

data.title: Largest IPOs of the last 90 days register mixed debut
data.description:
The global IPO market has seen a wave of high-value listings over the past three months, with the 10 largest offerings collectively raising billions of dollars across sectors including energy, fintech, real estate, industrial technology, and healthcare. However, post-listing performance reveals a divided market, with a majority of these newly public companies trading below their issue price.

Strong Fundraising, Uneven Returns

Leading the pack, Forgent Power Solutions (FPS) raised $1.74B in its February debut on the NYSE, pricing shares at $27. The offering included both primary and secondary shares, with proceeds partly used to restructure ownership interests tied to Neos-controlled entities. The stock has since gained 7%, reflecting modest investor confidence.

PayPay (PAYP) followed with a $1.01B IPO, marking the largest U.S. listing by a Japanese firm in over a decade. Despite pricing its ADRs below the marketed range at $16, the fintech company has delivered strong aftermarket performance, rising 31% and standing out as the top performer among recent listings.

Janus Living (JAN), a senior housing REIT raised $878M and has climbed 19.5% since its debut. The company plans to deploy proceeds toward acquisitions and expansion within the senior living sector.

SOLV Energy (MWH) has been one of the brighter spots, with shares up 18.9% following its IPO. The company’s positioning in the energy infrastructure space appears to have resonated with investors amid ongoing demand for energy transition assets.

Early Gains Fade for Some

EquipmentShare.com (EQPT), a construction technology and rental platform, initially surged nearly 20% on its market debut but has since reversed course, now trading 17% below its IPO price.

Similarly, York Space Systems (YSS), which raised $629M in an upsized offering, has slipped 17.6% despite early trading gains.

Healthcare IPOs Face Significant Pressure

Healthcare and biotech companies have experienced the steepest declines among recent IPOs. MiniMed Group (MMED), a diabetes care business spun out of Medtronic (MDT), is down nearly 30% since listing.

Generate Biomedicines (GENB) has fallen 14.1% since its IPO, while Eikon Therapeutics (EIKN) has dropped sharply by 45% at $9.81, compared to its IPO price of $18, making it the worst performer on the list.

PicS N.V. (PICS), a Brazilian fintech and mobile banking platform, has also struggled, declining 43.5% despite reporting strong profit growth ahead of its IPO.

Market Trends and Outlook

Overall, 6 out of the 10 largest IPOs in the past 90 days are currently trading below their offering price, underscoring a cautious public market environment. While companies have successfully raised capital, investors appear more selective, particularly penalizing firms with longer paths to profitability or higher perceived risk.

Sector performance has been uneven, with energy and fintech showing relative strength, while healthcare—especially biotech—has faced significant headwinds.

data.title: Tom Brady and SharkNinja want to make boring backyards the GOAT with Upgrady ad campaign
data.description:
Seven-time Super Bowl champ Tom Brady is helping SharkNinja (SN) sell barbeque grills, pizza ovens, and coolers with a new “Tom Upgrady” ad campaign to encourage families to improve their outdoor space.

To kick off the campaign, SharkNinja’s Tom Upgrady van surprised Boston-area homeowners with a “backyard upgrade” with merchandise from SharkNinja’s outdoor portfolio and face time with the MVP quarterback.

Citing data from the ICFA Outdoor Living Trend Report, SharkNinja (SN) claims 77% of U.S. households with outdoor space are not using it as much as they’d like.

“Tom Upgrady aims to change that by bringing high-performance outdoor products and inspiration directly to homeowners to show what’s possible with the right setup,” the company said.

The ad campaign continues through the summer, inviting customers to compete for the “MVP treatment” by sharing their backyard setups.
